summary: I work as a weekend radio host at Classical King FM 98.1, where I curate and host shows focused on classical and contemporary music. Before the COVID-19 pandemic, my days involved reviewing new music releases, curating playlists, and producing shows like Second Inversion. During the pandemic, the station implemented strict safety protocols, and although some staff worked remotely, I still went into the station regularly to access equipment and the music library. The leadership, especially under CEO Brenda Barnes, ensured a safe working environment, and the station remained on air 24/7 as a vital community resource.

Second Inversion is a contemporary and experimental music show I curate and host on Saturday nights at Classical King FM. The show officially launched in February 2020 with a listening party at The Rendezvous in Belltown, Seattle, just weeks before the pandemic shutdowns began. Before that, Second Inversion existed as a 24/7 online stream, but the launch on the main channel helped it reach a wider audience. Over the past year, especially during COVID, the show has grown significantly, and I've developed my hosting skills through this focused weekly program.

The COVID-19 pandemic changed how I work at Classical King FM, with many colleagues working remotely while I split time between home and the station. The station adopted strict safety measures like mask-wearing, sanitizing, and HVAC improvements to keep staff safe. Despite the challenges, the pandemic gave me flexibility and a chance to focus deeply on my show, Second Inversion, which launched just before the shutdowns. The station chose not to focus on COVID on air, providing listeners with a refuge through classical music during a stressful time.

Brenda Barnes is the CEO of Classical King FM and has been instrumental in guiding the station through the COVID-19 pandemic. She stays up to date on all the latest COVID protocols and participates in weekly meetings with UW Medicine and other experts to ensure workplace safety. Her leadership has been reassuring for me and my colleagues, as she and the management team provide clear instructions and are open to concerns about coming into the station during these times.

UW Medicine has played a role in helping Classical King FM maintain workplace safety during the COVID-19 pandemic. Our CEO, Brenda Barnes, attends weekly meetings with people from UW Medicine to stay informed about the best COVID protocols. Their guidance has helped us implement effective safety measures at the station, such as sanitation and HVAC improvements, so that we can continue broadcasting safely.

Dave Beck is a longtime host at King FM who indirectly helped me start my career in radio. When I was studying music and journalism at Seattle University, I met his partner who was a substitute teacher in one of my classes. She encouraged me to reach out to Dave, who kindly showed me around the station and introduced me to the Second Inversion team. That connection led to my internship and eventually a full-time job at King FM.

I attended Seattle University where I studied music and journalism. It was there that I had a substitute teacher who was the partner of Dave Beck from King FM, which led me to connect with the station. During my junior year, I was involved in music journalism and the arts and entertainment section of the school newspaper. This university experience was pivotal in launching my radio career.

I spent a summer in Paris studying contemporary experimental music composition as part of a program abroad. This was around the same time that the Second Inversion project was getting started at King FM. After finishing my composition program in Paris, I returned and began my internship with Second Inversion, which helped launch my career in radio hosting and music curation.

The Rendezvous in Belltown, Seattle, was the venue for the Second Inversion listening party in early February 2020. It was a great event where many people came together to celebrate the launch of the show on King FM's main channel. Just weeks later, everything shut down due to the pandemic, so that party was a memorable moment before the world changed.

Belltown is a neighborhood in Seattle where The Rendezvous is located, the place where we held the Second Inversion listening party in February 2020. It was a lively gathering spot for music lovers just before the COVID-19 shutdowns began. Belltown has been part of my story as a place connected to the launch of my show and my work at King FM.

I play violin and have to travel to the University of Washington about twice a week to rehearse and perform with the Seattle Symphony. Being able to play in person during the pandemic has been a welcome escape from the COVID reality. The Seattle Symphony is an important part of my musical life and connects me to the broader classical music community in the city.

I go to the University of Washington regularly to play violin with the Seattle Symphony, usually about twice a week. The campus is part of my routine as a musician, and even during the pandemic, being able to attend rehearsals there has been a vital part of maintaining my connection to music. The University of Washington is a key place in my life as both a performer and a community member.
